---
title: "Semantics and the canonical term model"
qmd-prover:
  imports:
    - from: foundations.qmd
      use:
        - def-fol-formulas
        - def-fol-signature
        - def-fol-substitution
        - def-fol-terms
        - lem-substitution-composition
    - from: calculus.qmd
      use:
        - def-consistency
        - def-hilbert-calculus
        - lem-complete-theory-boolean
---

::: {#def-fol-structure .definition name="Structures and assignments" date="2026-07-12"}
For a signature \(L\) from @def-fol-signature, an \(L\)-structure
\(\mathcal M\) has a nonempty domain \(M\), interprets every
\(f\in\mathcal F_n\) as a function \(M^n\to M\), and every
\(R\in\mathcal R_n\) as a subset of \(M^n\).  Logical equality is interpreted
as actual equality on \(M\).  An assignment is a function
\(s:\mathrm{Var}\to M\) on the variables \(\mathrm{Var}\) of @def-fol-terms;
for \(x\in\mathrm{Var}\) and \(a\in M\) write \(s[x\mapsto a]\) for the
assignment agreeing with \(s\) except that it sends \(x\) to \(a\).
:::

::: {#def-term-value .definition name="Value of a term" date="2026-07-12"}
For @def-fol-structure and the terms of @def-fol-terms, the value
\(\llbracket t\rrbracket^{\mathcal M}_s\in M\) is defined by recursion on the
structure of \(t\): \(\llbracket x\rrbracket^{\mathcal M}_s=s(x)\) for a variable
\(x\); \(\llbracket c\rrbracket^{\mathcal M}_s=c^{\mathcal M}\) for a constant
\(c\) (the value of the nullary function symbol \(c\)); and
\[
\llbracket f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\rrbracket^{\mathcal M}_s
=f^{\mathcal M}(\llbracket t_1\rrbracket^{\mathcal M}_s,\ldots,
\llbracket t_n\rrbracket^{\mathcal M}_s).
\]
Since @def-fol-terms presents terms as an inductive structure, this recursion
assigns a unique value to every term under every assignment.
:::

::: {#def-satisfaction .definition name="Satisfaction" date="2026-07-12" export="def-satisfaction"}
For @def-fol-formulas, @def-fol-structure, and @def-term-value, the relation
\(\mathcal M,s\models A\) between a structure with an assignment and a formula is
defined by recursion on \(A\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal M,s\models R(\bar t)&\iff
 (\llbracket\bar t\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M})\in R^{\mathcal M},\\
\mathcal M,s\models t=u&\iff
 \llbracket t\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M}=\llbracket u\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M},\\
\mathcal M,s\models\neg A&\iff\mathcal M,s\not\models A,\\
\mathcal M,s\models A\wedge B&\iff
 \mathcal M,s\models A\text{ and }\mathcal M,s\models B,\\
\mathcal M,s\models\exists xA&\iff
 \text{some }a\in M\text{ satisfies }\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto a]\models A.
\end{aligned}
\]
For a sentence \(A\), write \(\mathcal M\models A\) to mean that
\(\mathcal M,s\models A\) holds for every assignment \(s\).  Write
\(\mathcal M\models\Gamma\) when \(\mathcal M\models B\) for every \(B\in\Gamma\),
and, for a set \(\Gamma\) of \(L\)-sentences and an \(L\)-sentence \(A\), write
\(\Gamma\models_L A\) when every \(L\)-structure \(\mathcal M\) with
\(\mathcal M\models\Gamma\) has \(\mathcal M\models A\).
:::

::: {#lem-coincidence .lemma name="Coincidence lemma" date="2026-07-12"}
For @def-term-value and @def-satisfaction: if assignments \(s,s'\) agree on
\(\mathrm{Var}(t)\) then \(\llbracket t\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M}=\llbracket t
\rrbracket_{s'}^{\mathcal M}\); and if \(s,s'\) agree on \(\mathrm{FV}(A)\) then
\(\mathcal M,s\models A\) iff \(\mathcal M,s'\models A\).  In particular a closed
term has the same value under every assignment, and the truth of a sentence is
the same under every assignment.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-coincidence"}
The term statement is a structural induction on @def-fol-terms using the
evaluation clauses of @def-term-value: a variable \(x\in\mathrm{Var}(t)\) has
value \(s(x)=s'(x)\); a constant's value \(c^{\mathcal M}\) does not mention the
assignment; and for \(f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\) each
\(\mathrm{Var}(t_i)\subseteq\mathrm{Var}(t)\), so the induction hypothesis gives
equal argument values, hence equal values by @def-term-value.  The
formula statement is a structural induction on @def-fol-formulas using the free
variables fixed there.  An atomic formula depends by @def-satisfaction only on
the values of its terms, whose variables lie in \(\mathrm{FV}(A)\), so the term
statement applies; \(\neg A\) and \(A\wedge B\) follow from the induction
hypothesis since \(\mathrm{FV}(\neg A)=\mathrm{FV}(A)\) and
\(\mathrm{FV}(A\wedge B)=\mathrm{FV}(A)\cup\mathrm{FV}(B)\).  For \(\exists xB\),
by @def-satisfaction each side holds iff \(\mathcal M,s[x\mapsto a]\models B\)
for some \(a\in M\) (respectively with \(s'\)); since \(s,s'\) agree on
\(\mathrm{FV}(\exists xB)=\mathrm{FV}(B)\setminus\{x\}\), the updated assignments
\(s[x\mapsto a]\) and \(s'[x\mapsto a]\) agree on \(\mathrm{FV}(B)\), so the
induction hypothesis equates the two conditions witness by witness.  A closed
term and a sentence have empty variable and free-variable sets, on which any two
assignments trivially agree.
:::

::: {#lem-semantic-substitution .lemma name="Semantic substitution lemma" date="2026-07-12"}
If \(t\) is free for \(x\) in \(A\), then
\[
 \mathcal M,s\models A[t/x]
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
 \mathcal M,s[x\mapsto\llbracket t\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M}]\models A.
\]
The corresponding term-value identity also holds.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-semantic-substitution"}
First the term identity: for every term \(w\),
\(\llbracket w[t/x]\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M}=\llbracket w\rrbracket_{s'}^{\mathcal M}\)
with \(s'=s[x\mapsto\llbracket t\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M}]\), by induction on
\(w\) via @def-fol-substitution and @def-term-value: \(x[t/x]=t\) gives
\(\llbracket t\rrbracket_s\) on both sides, another variable or a constant is
fixed and has the same value under \(s\) and \(s'\), and a function term reduces
to its arguments.  Now induct on \(A\) via @def-fol-formulas.  Atomic cases use
the term identity and @def-satisfaction; \(\neg\) and \(\wedge\) use the
induction hypothesis.  For \(\exists yB\), with \(t\) free for \(x\) in
\(\exists yB\): if \(x\notin\mathrm{FV}(\exists yB)\) — in particular when
\(y=x\), or when \(x\notin\mathrm{FV}(B)\) — then \((\exists yB)[t/x]=\exists yB\)
by @def-fol-substitution, while \(s\) and \(s'\) differ only at \(x\), which is
not free in \(\exists yB\); so @lem-coincidence gives them the same truth value
and the equivalence is immediate.  Otherwise \(y\ne x\) and
\(x\in\mathrm{FV}(B)\), so the free-for hypothesis forces
\(y\notin\mathrm{Var}(t)\) and \((\exists yB)[t/x]=\exists y(B[t/x])\).  Fix
\(a\in M\).  Since
\(y\notin\mathrm{Var}(t)\), @lem-coincidence gives \(\llbracket t
\rrbracket_{s[y\mapsto a]}^{\mathcal M}=\llbracket t\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M}\),
and as \(x\ne y\) the updates commute:
\(s[y\mapsto a][x\mapsto\llbracket t\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M}]=s'[y\mapsto a]\).
The induction hypothesis for \(B\) at \(s[y\mapsto a]\) then gives
\(\mathcal M,s[y\mapsto a]\models B[t/x]\) iff \(\mathcal M,s'[y\mapsto a]\models
B\).  Quantifying over \(a\) and using @def-satisfaction yields the equivalence
for \(\exists yB\).
:::

::: {#def-henkin-theory .definition name="Henkin theory" date="2026-07-12" export="def-henkin-theory"}
A set \(T\) of sentences in a language with at least one constant is a
*Henkin theory* if, for every sentence \(\exists xA(x)\), there is a closed
term \(t\) such that
\(\exists xA(x)\to A(t)\in T\).  Such a \(t\) is a witness term.
:::

::: {#lem-equality-congruence .lemma name="Provable equality is a congruence" date="2026-07-12"}
Let \(T\) be deductively closed and consistent.  On closed terms put
\(t\sim_Tu\) iff \(t=u\in T\).  Then \(\sim_T\) is an equivalence relation,
is preserved by every function symbol, and replacement of \(\sim_T\)-equal
arguments preserves membership in \(T\) of every atomic relation formula.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-equality-congruence"}
The equality axioms in @def-hilbert-calculus give \(t=t\) and, by taking
appropriate instances of the replacement schema, symmetry
\(t=u\to u=t\), transitivity
\((t=u\wedge u=v)\to t=v\), function congruence, and relation
substitutivity.  Apply modus ponens and deductive closure from
@def-consistency.  Repeating one coordinate at a time proves the assertions
for all finite arities.  Thus the relation and interpretations below do not
depend on representatives.
:::

::: {#def-canonical-term-model .definition name="Canonical term model" date="2026-07-12" export="def-canonical-term-model"}
Let \(T\) be a deductively closed consistent theory in a language \(L^*\)
with a constant.  Its canonical term model \(\mathcal M_T\) has as domain the
\(\sim_T\)-classes \([t]\) of closed \(L^*\)-terms, using
@lem-equality-congruence.  Define
\[
 f^{\mathcal M_T}([t_1],\ldots,[t_n])=[f(t_1,\ldots,t_n)]
\]
and
\[
 ([t_1],\ldots,[t_n])\in R^{\mathcal M_T}
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad R(t_1,\ldots,t_n)\in T.
\]
The domain is nonempty because the language has a constant, and
@lem-equality-congruence makes all interpretations well-defined.
:::

::: {#lem-term-model-evaluation .lemma name="Evaluation by closed representatives" date="2026-07-12"}
Choose for every variable \(x\) a closed term \(r_x\), and define the
assignment \(s(x)=[r_x]\) in @def-canonical-term-model.  For every term
\(t(x_1,\ldots,x_k)\),
\[
 \llbracket t\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M_T}
   =[t[r_{x_1}/x_1,\ldots,r_{x_k}/x_k]].
\]
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-term-model-evaluation"}
Structural induction on @def-fol-terms.  For a variable \(x\), @def-term-value
gives \(\llbracket x\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M_T}=s(x)=[r_x]\), which is
\([x[r_{x_1}/x_1,\ldots,r_{x_k}/x_k]]\) because that substitution sends \(x\) to
\(r_x\).  For a constant \(c\), @def-term-value gives
\(\llbracket c\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M_T}=c^{\mathcal M_T}\), which equals
\([c]\) by the nullary case of the function interpretation in
@def-canonical-term-model, while \(c[r_{x_1}/x_1,\ldots]=c\) by
@def-fol-substitution, so both sides are \([c]\).  For \(f(t_1,\ldots,t_m)\),
the function interpretation in @def-canonical-term-model and the induction
hypothesis give
\(\llbracket f(t_1,\ldots,t_m)\rrbracket_s^{\mathcal M_T}
=f^{\mathcal M_T}([t_1[\vec r/\vec x]],\ldots,[t_m[\vec r/\vec x]])
=[f(t_1,\ldots,t_m)[r_{x_1}/x_1,\ldots,r_{x_k}/x_k]]\).  Every substituted term
is closed by @def-fol-terms, so @def-fol-substitution performs no renaming.
:::

::: {#lem-existential-membership .lemma name="Existential membership in a complete Henkin theory" date="2026-07-12"}
If \(T\) is consistent, deductively closed, negation-complete, and Henkin,
then for every formula \(A(x)\) whose free variables are among \(\{x\}\),
\[
 \exists xA(x)\in T
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
 A(t)\in T\text{ for some closed term }t.
\]
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-existential-membership"}
For left-to-right, choose the witness term supplied by @def-henkin-theory and
use modus ponens plus deductive closure from @def-consistency.  Conversely,
quantifier axiom 5 in @def-hilbert-calculus directly gives
\(A(t)\to\exists xA\) (a closed term is free for \(x\)).  Modus ponens and
deductive closure put the existential in \(T\).
:::

::: {#lem-canonical-truth .lemma name="Canonical truth lemma" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-canonical-truth"}
Let \(T\) be a consistent, deductively closed, negation-complete Henkin theory.
Choose closed representatives \(r_x\) and the corresponding assignment \(s\)
as in @lem-term-model-evaluation.  For every formula \(A\) whose free
variables are among \(x_1,\ldots,x_k\),
\[
 \mathcal M_T,s\models A
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
 A[r_{x_1}/x_1,\ldots,r_{x_k}/x_k]\in T.
\]
In particular, for every sentence \(A\),
\(\mathcal M_T\models A\) iff \(A\in T\).
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-canonical-truth"}
Induct on the formula \(A\) generated by @def-fol-formulas, proving the
biconditional *uniformly in the closed representatives*: the induction
hypothesis for a subformula is that the biconditional holds for every family of
closed representatives and its assignment from @lem-term-model-evaluation, not
only the fixed family \((r_x)\).  For relation atoms, use
@lem-term-model-evaluation and the relation interpretation in
@def-canonical-term-model; for equality atoms, the two evaluated classes are
equal exactly when the substituted equality belongs to \(T\), by
@lem-equality-congruence.  Negation and conjunction follow from
@def-satisfaction and @lem-complete-theory-boolean.

For \(\exists xB\), whose free variables lie among \(x_1,\ldots,x_k\), let
\(x_{i_1},\ldots,x_{i_m}\) be those \(x_i\) different from \(x\) and set
\(A(x):=B[r_{x_{i_1}}/x_{i_1},\ldots,r_{x_{i_m}}/x_{i_m}]\), a formula whose only
possible free variable is \(x\).  The closed representatives leave every binder
in place, and \(x\) is not free in \(\exists xB\), so by @def-fol-substitution
\((\exists xB)[r_{x_1}/x_1,\ldots,r_{x_k}/x_k]=\exists x\,A(x)\): substitution for
an \(x_i\) equal to \(x\) is vacuous and the remaining substitutions pass under
the binder.  By @def-satisfaction, \(\mathcal M_T,s\models\exists xB\) holds
exactly when \(\mathcal M_T,s[x\mapsto d]\models B\) for some domain element
\(d\); every \(d\) is \([t]\) for a closed term \(t\) by
@def-canonical-term-model, and \(s[x\mapsto[t]]\) is the assignment of the
representative family agreeing with \((r_x)\) except that it sends \(x\) to
\(t\).  The induction hypothesis at that family gives \(\mathcal
M_T,s[x\mapsto[t]]\models B\) iff \(B[t/x,r_{x_{i_1}}/x_{i_1},\ldots,r_{x_{i_m}}
/x_{i_m}]\in T\), and by @lem-substitution-composition this closed instance is
\(A(x)[t/x]\).  Hence \(\mathcal M_T,s\models\exists xB\) iff \(A(x)[t/x]\in T\)
for some closed \(t\), which by @lem-existential-membership is equivalent to
\(\exists x\,A(x)\in T\), i.e. to
\((\exists xB)[r_{x_1}/x_1,\ldots,r_{x_k}/x_k]\in T\).  This completes the
induction.  For a sentence \(A\) (\(k=0\)) the biconditional reads
\(\mathcal M_T,s\models A\) iff \(A\in T\); by @lem-coincidence the truth of a
sentence is independent of \(s\), so \(\mathcal M_T\models A\) iff \(A\in T\).
:::

::: {#lem-reduct-satisfaction .lemma name="Reduct preserves old-language truth" date="2026-07-12" export="lem-reduct-satisfaction"}
If \(L\subseteq L^*\), \(\mathcal N\) is an \(L^*\)-structure, and
\(\mathcal N\upharpoonright L\) is obtained by forgetting the new-symbol
interpretations, then every \(L\)-formula has the same truth value in
\(\mathcal N\) and in its reduct under the same assignment.
:::

::: {.proof of="lem-reduct-satisfaction"}
Term induction using @def-term-value shows equal values because all symbols of
an \(L\)-term retain their interpretations.  Formula induction using
@def-satisfaction then gives the claim: atomic cases use those term values and
unchanged relations, Boolean cases use the induction hypothesis, and the
existential case quantifies over the same nonempty domain.
:::
